title: Cambridge School Leader Standards

source.format: Single-level standard statements (no developmental ladder by design — same convention as the Teacher Standards)

purpose: Define the range of characteristics and practices school leaders need to effectively provide Cambridge programmes. Used for self-evaluation, professional development, and governance accountability — not formal accreditation.
audiences: Senior school leaders (heads / principals) for self-evaluation and CPD
Aspiring school leaders mapping the role's scope
School boards and governing bodies for recruitment, retention and accountability oversight
Distributed-leadership roles: pastoral heads, heads of subject departments, curriculum coordinators (subset of Standards applies)
scopeNote: The Standards include school leaders' responsibilities for effective management systems, but exclude management responsibilities outside scope (managing facilities, HR, fund-raising, development) — those are addressed in the Cambridge Standards for Schools.
distributedLeadership: Cambridge is committed to distributed leadership — both responsibility and delegated authority for leading specific aspects of the school's work are distributed among staff. Pastoral heads, heads of subject departments, curriculum coordinators and others should treat these Standards as relevant to their work.

domains.name: Leadership values and attributes
domains.summary: Provide direction and vision; model wellbeing, ethical conduct, distributed leadership and reflective lifelong learning.

standards.text: provide a strong sense of direction and a clear vision for the school
prioritise the physical, emotional, and psychological wellbeing of students and staff, ensuring a safe and healthy environment
model the highest standards of ethical conduct in their interactions with the school community
empower staff by distributing professional responsibilities and establishing shared accountability
model a commitment to reflective practice and lifelong learning

domains.name: School culture and environment
domains.summary: Establish a high-expectation, fair, inclusive culture; build relationships across the community; nurture teacher accountability for learning; partner with external institutions.

standards.text: establish a culture of high expectations for all, in regard to learning outcomes, behaviours and attitudes
demonstrate emotional intelligence and empathy in leadership to foster a fair and inclusive environment
establish professional relationships with all members of the school community
motivate and encourage teachers to take responsibility for the development of effective learning and teaching
enrich the learning culture of their school by establishing partnerships with other institutions, professional bodies and educational organisations

domains.name: Leadership skills and practices
domains.summary: Stay research-informed on leadership and on curriculum/pedagogy/assessment; maintain policies + procedures; develop staff and the leadership team; plan and budget annually.

standards.text: demonstrate knowledge and understanding of current research into educational leadership and the practices of effective leaders, and use it to support the reflective evaluation of their own practice
keep up to date with the latest research and evidence into effective curriculum, pedagogy and assessment
make sure that the school has appropriate policies and procedures in place, as well as written roles and responsibilities, which are reflected in operational practice
demonstrate a consistent commitment to the professional learning and development of all staff, including the development of their leadership potential
plan and budget for the annual cycle informed by evidence, data and curriculum requirements
develop a leadership team that meets the school's operational and developmental needs

domains.name: Innovation and improvement through leadership
domains.summary: Lead change; foster a collaborative innovation culture; allocate time and resources for evidence-based improvement; review staff performance; share effective practice; share whole-school responsibility for the improvement plan.

standards.text: understand the principles and practices of school improvement, leadership and management of change to enable the school to grow and flourish
create a professional culture in which innovation and collaboration for the improvement of learning and teaching practice are encouraged
allocate time and resources for the evaluation and improvement planning process that is based on data and stakeholder input
establish and review documented procedures that evaluate staff performance and inform professional development needs
promote and model collaboration among all staff to evaluate and share effective practice and to develop content knowledge and pedagogy
establish whole-school responsibility for the implementation, monitoring and evaluation of the improvement plan

domains.name: Community engagement
domains.summary: Build wider-community partnerships, involve parents and carers, and inspire student citizenship.

standards.text: actively engage with the wider community to build strong partnerships and collaborative relationships that support and reflect the purpose and values of the school
promote and facilitate the involvement of parents and carers in the life of the school
inspire student participation in the wider community, contributing to their development as responsible, active citizens

rationaleByDomain.1: Values that inform leadership for learning are moral purpose, professional integrity and critical friendship (MacBeath et al., 2018; MacBeath & Dempster, 2009; Swaffield & MacBeath, 2013). Effective school leaders model trust in their relationships within their own schools and with leaders of partner schools (Hargreaves, 2012). Effective school leaders need to model a consistent commitment to reflective practice and lifelong learning (OECD, 2019) and demonstrate a consistent commitment to well-being in all its forms (OECD, 2021; European Commission, 2021).
rationaleByDomain.2: A school's purpose and mission serve as the bedrock of its culture (Deal & Peterson, 2016). School leaders must demonstrate emotional intelligence and empathy because the school climate is a determinant of resilience and well-being (Cohen, 2013). Effective school leaders motivate and encourage teachers to take accountability for the development of effective learning and teaching (European Commission, 2021). Schools don't operate in a vacuum; they are 'open systems' (OECD, 2016).
rationaleByDomain.3: Seminal evidence points to four categories of leadership practices used by successful leaders: setting directions, developing people, redesigning the organisation and managing the instructional programme (Leithwood, 2013). Effective school leaders demonstrate knowledge and understanding of current research into educational leadership and use it to support the reflective evaluation of their own practice (OECD, 2019). Evidence strongly points to school leaders improving teaching and learning indirectly and most powerfully through their influence on staff motivation, ability and working conditions (Leithwood et al., 2020).
rationaleByDomain.4: School leaders can strongly influence the creation of a professional culture in which innovation and collaboration for the improvement of learning and teaching practice are encouraged. Effective leaders realise the knowledge and expertise possessed by others and model and promote purposeful collaborative professionalism (Hargreaves & O'Connor, 2018). They expand this beyond individuals and promote productive collaboration between teams to evaluate and share effective practice. Meaningful change is only possible with leadership and commitment at all levels (Braun, 2008).
rationaleByDomain.5: Whether schools are a welcoming place for parents and whether there are strong connections between the school and local institutions have been linked to improvement in student attainment for decades (MacBeath & Mortimore, 2001; UNESCO, 2021). Schools with strong parental involvement are ten times more likely to improve in math and four times more likely to improve in reading than schools weak on this measure (Bryk et al., 2010). School leaders should forge partnerships with parents and members of the local community (OECD, 2016).
